title: Alert Process
content: Alert Process How AwareNet detects, receives, and responds to alerts. 1 The camera automatically detects a person moving and immediately sends an email or webhook to AwareNet that includes the video clip it recorded. 2 AwareNet automatically receives the email or webhook, verifies it is from an enrolled camera, and immediately displays the alert to the person designated by the neighborhood to monitor the alerts (safety committee volunteer or paid security personnel). 3 The people receiving the alerts review the video clip and determine what, if any, action should be taken (e.g. call 911).

title: Camera Configuration
content: Camera Configuration Select your camera brand for configuration instructions: Reolink Configuration instructions for Reolink cameras Axis Configuration instructions for Axis cameras

title: Camera Location
content: Camera Location After you have completed the Getting Started steps, you are ready to physically install/mount your camera in the desired location. Here are some tips: Battery-powered cameras (like the Reolink Argus 3 Pro) typically have a maximum range of 25-30 feet for sensing motion (they use "passive infrared" sensors). So we recommend placing this type of camera as close as possible to the street or alley (an adjacent pole, tree or garage). Outlet-powered cameras and power-over-ethernet cameras typically have a longer maximum range (30-60 feet, or more) for sensing motion (they use software to "see" changes in the picture). So we recommend placing this type of camera on an exterior wall of your house or garage. Generally, cameras sense objects moving side-to-side in the field of view better than objects moving straight towards or away from the camera. So for all cameras, we recommend pointing the camera roughly perpendicular to the street or alley (straight across to the other side). For all cameras, we recommend placing them 8-15 feet above the street or alley. Each situation is unique, but generally, higher than 15 feet will likely reduce the capability of the camera. If you are using a camera that relies on light coming from the camera (regular or infra-red), be sure the leading edge of the camera extends a bit beyond any nearby wall or object so the light is not reflected back into the camera lens. Landscape lighting pointed at the camera will typically cause the camera picture to be darker than normal. Wall lighting that is level with the camera can also cause the camera picture to be darker than normal. Generally, it is best to position the camera above or below the wall light so the light fixture is not in the camera's field of view. Either way, a night time test is recommend before permanent installation. NOTE: For Reolink cameras, the mounting screws they include are adequate for drywall or other light-weight material, but you will snap off the screw heads if you use them for wood without drilling a proper pilot hole. Having at least one street camera per block is the goal. Depending on the distance of the camera from the street, or for wide streets, it may be best to have one camera on each side of the street. Having at least one alley camera per block would complete the coverage.

title: Camera Recording
content: Camera Recording Motion Recording Schedule Control what gets saved to your data card. Detection Type - Turn off "Any Motion". It will record bushes and trees blown by the wind, changing shadows, etc. Turn on Person and Vehicle. Animal is optional. Days and Hours - Enable (paint blue) 24 x 7 so your camera records activity even during hours that AwareNet is not active. Your setup should look like the screenshot below. Post-recording Duration Set this to 15 seconds unless you have a reason to set it higher. Setting it higher will reduce how many days of recording are retained. Continuous Recording Schedule Not recommended unless you are doing it for a very short duration or have your camera configured to use an NVR (network video recorder) instead of a data card.

content: Customers Neighborhoods protected by AwareNet. Flora Place St. Louis, MO Cathedral Square St. Louis, MO Saint Louis Hills St. Louis, MO Washington Pl & Westminster - Lake St. Louis, MO

title: Detection Alarm
content: Detection Alarm Person Object Size - use only as a last resort for fixing false alerts. Sensitivity - increase if the camera is failing to detect person motion. Decrease if the camera is detecting people when there are none (false person alert). Alarm Delay - set this to 2 seconds. Vehicle Object Size - use only as a last resort for fixing false alerts. Sensitivity - increase if the camera is failing to detect vehicle motion. Decrease if the camera is detecting vehicles when there are none (false vehicle alert). Alarm Delay - set this to 1 second. Animal Not used for AwareNet. Motion Not used for AwareNet. Non-Detection Zone Configuration for areas to exclude from detection. Helps minimize false alerts. Paint the areas that the camera can ignore .

title: Email Alerts
content: Email Alerts Configure this section carefully to ensure your camera sends the correct email alerts to AwareNet. Alarm Schedule Control what gets sent to AwareNet. Detection Type - Turn on only "Person" and "Vehicle" (turn off "Any Motion" and "Animal") Days and Hours - Enable (paint blue) midnight to 6am and 10pm to midnight, seven days a week. Your setup should look like the screenshot below. Timer Schedule Do not use! This feature will send email alerts based on elapsed time rather than detected motion. Email Settings Sender's Email - Contact your neighborhood support person to get the correct email address and password SMTP Server - Enter "smtp.gmail.com" Server Port - Enter "465" SSL or TLS - Turn on Recipient(s) - Contact your neighborhood support person to get the correct email address. Do not use the Sender email address as a recipient. Email Content Set to "Text with Video". Email Interval Set to "30 seconds".

title: Enroll a Camera
content: Enroll a Camera Be sure to complete the prior steps listed on the Getting Started page before enrolling. This will ensure you have the camera name and email address you need to complete this enrollment form. Also, please check the FAQ page if you have questions about using your camera with AwareNet. Enroll a Camera

title: False Alerts
content: False Alerts Issues and Fixes Reolink False Alerts occur when: The camera thinks it detects a person, but there is no actual person for the camera to detect. The camera thinks it detects a moving vehicle, but there is no actual moving vehicle for the camera to detect. The original Duo cameras can be especially prone to false vehicle alerts, whereas the Duo 2, Duo 3, and other newer Reolink cameras are much improved. The first thing to do is to review video clips from your camera. To do this, go to the live view from your camera and then select the "Playback" button. This will allow you to watch video clips for the current day. Select a date from the calendar to watch videos for a different day. In the video list, each clip is marked with one or more symbols to indicate what was detected (person, vehicle, etc.). Based on a review of the video clips, look for the matching cause listed below to see the suggested solution. If you cannot determine what object has activated the camera, try turning on the Motion Boxes feature which will highlight objects during Clear (High) resolution playback. Settings, Display, Show Motion Boxes . If the listed solutions do not work or are not available in your camera settings, try updating the firmware for your camera . Spider webs or other debris obscuring the view of the camera › Solution: Gently clean the glass cover protecting the camera lens. If spider webs are a frequent issue, try turning off the infrared lights (which attract spiders). Settings, Light, Infrared Lights, Close (off) Note - When the infrared lights are turned off, it is usually beneficial to also set the camera to always color mode. Settings, Display, Day and Night, Switch Mode, Color Note - When the always color mode is used and the area is dark at night, it may be beneficial to set the spotlight / floodlight to Timer Mode. Settings, Light, Spotlight, Modes, Timer Mode Yard decorations that look like the shape of a person or vehicle › Solution: If practical, try making the area with the decorations a non-detection area. Settings, Detection Alarm, Non-Detection Zone Or, increase the alarm delay for detecting. Settings, Detection Alarm, Person/Vehicle, Alarm Delay. Increase 1 second. Flags that are blowing in the wind (especially ones that partially obscure the view of a parked vehicle) › Solution: Increase the alarm delay for detecting. Settings, Detection Alarm, Person/Vehicle, Alarm Delay. Increase 1 second. Or, reduce sensitivity. Settings, Detection Alarm, Person/Vehicle, Sensitivity. Decrease 5 points. Do not go lower than 50 for Person or lower than 30 for Vehicle Strong / gusting wind › Solution: If practical, trim back the bush, ivy, or tree that is causing the issue. Or, it may be the camera is not securely mounted and the wind is causing the camera to move which can make the camera think that objects are moving. If so, mount the camera more securely. If the problem still occurs frequently, try the suggestions listed for "Flags that are blowing in the wind". Heavy rain or snow › Solution: This issue is resolved when the precipitation is reduced or stops, so there is typically no need to make any changes. But if this occurs too often, try the suggestions listed for "Flags that are blowing in the wind". Flashing or blinking lights › Solution: If practical, try making the area with the flashing / blinking light a non-detection area. Settings, Detection Alarm, Non-Detection Zone Or, increase the alarm delay for detecting. Settings, Detection Alarm, Person/Vehicle, Alarm Delay. Increase 1 second.

title: Feature Summary
content: Feature Summary What AwareNet brings to your neighborhood. Consolidation and federation of automated alerts sent by otherwise standalone security cameras. Capacity to support hundreds of cameras working together to help keep your neighborhood safe. Compatible with dozens of security cameras costing less than $200 each, available on Amazon or directly from the camera manufacturer. Each camera owner maintains control of the privacy and security features for their camera. Easy-to-use software application for review and categorization of real-time alerts. Securely accessible via the internet and ideal for use on a tablet. Designated reviewer (e.g. security guard or officer) can assess real-time alerts and take immediate action when appropriate to disrupt and deter criminal activity. Important time stamps to help ensure the reviewer is processing alerts in a timely manner. GPS mapping to help ensure the patrol person is covering the full service area. False alerts are minimized with camera AI that identifies motion as activity by a person, a vehicle, or something else. Ability to disable alerts for a camera when it is sending too many alerts (e.g. block party, configuration issue, etc.). Map of all camera locations with status information for each camera and ability to re-enable alerts. Easy export of data for analysis and reporting using any spreadsheet app. See the FAQ page for answers to many common questions.

title: Getting Started
content: Getting Started Follow these steps to get your camera working with AwareNet: Verify your camera works with AwareNet or purchase one that is . Complete the basic configuration and setup for your camera before doing the physical installation. For Reolink, follow their Quick Start Guide included with the camera. [10 - 15 minutes]. You can do the physical installation of your camera now or wait until after Step 3. Complete the camera configuration steps for use with AwareNet. [10-15 minutes] Enroll your camera with AwareNet so AwareNet will recognize the email alerts from your camera. [5 minutes] You can do the physical installation of your camera now (if you didn't do it after Step 2). See the FAQ for answers to many commonly asked questions.

title: Our Team
content: Our Team The people behind AwareNet. Tom Scheifler Founder & President Tom Scheifler enjoyed a long and successful career as an independent software contractor. Tom designed, built, and deployed many enterprise-level, bespoke software solutions for industry leading corporations including Monsanto, HP, Boeing, Maritz, Pfizer, and FleishmanHillard. In 2021, after three years of increased property crime incidents in his home town, St. Louis, MO, coupled with significant decreases in police department staff, Tom created the initial version of AwareNet for use by his neighborhood. After successful testing and implementation for his neighborhood, Tom made AwareNet available to all St. Louis City neighborhoods, at no-profit, and volunteered his time to assist residents with neighborhood security initiatives. With AwareNet now in use by five different areas in St. Louis, Tom is inspired to expand the benefits of AwareNet throughout St. Louis and beyond. Glenn Weikert Director of Operations Since 2001, Glenn Weikert has owned and operated a company that builds full-stack and mobile software. His clients include the New York Knicks, the New York Rangers, Home Depot, Lucent Solar, who is a partner with Tesla and one of the top installers of only 26 certified companies to install Tesla Solar Roof, and IVI-RMA Global, the world's leading reproductive medicine group. Glenn was born and raised in Mechanicsburg, PA. He lived in Philadelphia for 10 years, Los Angeles for 13 years and has been in St Louis raising his daughter since 2022. He worked with dually diagnosed homeless men at the RHD Men's Homeless Shelter in Philadelphia. He visits children at the St Louis Children's Hospital every Thursday and volunteers at a Missouri Department of Corrections prison once a month. His step father is a retired police officer from Pennsylvania who served his community for 35 years. All of these experiences give Glenn a unique, compassionate perspective on the sometimes complicated relationship between citizens and police. Like Tom, Glenn saw a spike in crime in his St Louis neighborhood. There was an attempted abduction, two murders, one car jacking, two car thefts and numerous home and car break ins near his home in beautiful, popular Central West End of St Louis. He applied and was appointed to the seven member, Alderman and Mayor selected Washington Place Special District Board. While trying to develop his own camera system for Washington Place, he was introduced to Tom Scheifler and joined the team. He has dedicated himself to AwareNet because it's an inspiring, powerful tool that helps communities unite and keep our children and neighborhoods safe. CB Charles Betts Product Advisor Charles Betts served his community for 11 years until he retired from the St. Louis Metropolitan Police Department. In 2007, Charles established The City's Finest , a premier security services company for communities and businesses. Charles volunteered important guidance during the early development of AwareNet and his ongoing advice helps ensure AwareNet will continue to evolve and improve for our customers. Jim Whyte Product Advisor Jim Whyte served his community for 20+ years during his career with the St. Louis Metropolitan Police Department. In 2012, Jim transitioned to a new community-based role as Executive Director of the Central West End Neighborhood Security Initiative where he has collaborated with residents and businesses to create the safest possible environment. Jim's leadership in combining technology and human resources to benefit the area has been recognized on a regional and national level. Jim volunteered important guidance during the early development of AwareNet and his ongoing advice helps ensure AwareNet will continue to evolve and improve for our customers.

title: Pricing
content: Pricing Standard Pricing Standard pricing for AwareNet is $129 per month plus a minimum of $4.50 per camera per month (10pm to 6am, 7 days per week). St. Louis City Pricing For neighborhoods in St. Louis City, Missouri, we continue to provide AwareNet at a "no-profit" price. More Information For more information, please contact us .

title: Privacy Masking
content: Privacy Masking How to protect privacy using your camera's masking feature. Without Privacy Masking With Privacy Masking (set by camera owner)

title: Q&A
content: Q&A Tap a question to see the answer. Do cameras deter crime? › Generally, criminals are no longer deterred by security cameras and bright lights. They assume, based on their experience, that residential security cameras are not monitored for activity at night so no one will respond to disrupt their criminal activity. The AwareNet approach involves many cameras (i.e. at least one per block) with real-time monitoring and real-time response to disrupt criminal activity while it is occurring. Although success is not guaranteed and nothing deters all crime, we have proven the AwareNet approach can be successful. Is AwareNet a business? › Yes. AwareNet is available for any neighborhood in the United States. But the founder, Tom Scheifler, is committed to provide AwareNet at no profit to all 79 neighborhoods in the city of St. Louis (his hometown). Who pays for the service? › A neighborhood organization pays for the service by signing up as an AwareNet customer. After a neighborhood becomes a customer, a neighborhood resident becomes an AwareNet "member" (not a customer) when they enroll a camera with AwareNet. There is no cost to the resident to enroll a camera. Please contact us if you know of a business or organization that would like to sponsor the AwareNet service for your neighborhood! Who pays for the cameras? › Typically, residents of each block purchase their own cameras. However, we are always looking for ways to reduce or eliminate the cost of the camera for residents. Please contact us if you know of a business or organization that would like to sponsor cameras for your neighborhood! Are there terms of use available? › Yes. The terms of use are available for Customers and Members (camera owners) accessible on the site menu under Legal. Which cameras are supported by AwareNet? › See the Brands and Models information page. Please request support for other brands/models of cameras that meet the minimum requirements. We will work to support as many different cameras as possible. I already have home security cameras. Do I have to replace them to enroll a camera with AwareNet? › No. You can keep your existing home security cameras and simply buy a camera that is compatible with AwareNet. Who owns and operates the cameras? › Each resident or business owns and operates their camera(s). Residents can also work as a group to purchase and install cameras for their block. Is there a monthly "subscription" fee? › There is no cost to the camera owner to operate the camera (other than the nominal cost of the electric and internet usage). Many camera brands offer "premium" services for a monthly subscription fee that may be of interest to residents, but are not required for use with AwareNet. The neighborhood association, HOA, SBD, CID, etc. is the AwareNet customer and pays the monthly cost for the AwareNet service. Where can residents buy the cameras? › AwareNet does not sell cameras. The cameras are available for purchase at Reolink and Amazon . Does AwareNet configure and install cameras? › We can recommend a professional to do your installation and configuration instructions are available. Hopefully, each block has a "block buddy" who has some experience with DIY home security cameras and can be the person to install a camera or help someone else on the block. But please, do contact us if you have issues with configuration or installation and we'll try to help. Do I need a computer to setup or operate the camera? › No. You do not need a traditional laptop or desktop computer. But you do need an Android or Apple smartphone or tablet (and of course, you need home internet service too). How do I get started? Is there a cost? › Follow the steps outlined on the Getting Started page. You need to supply your own camera but the AwareNet service is paid for by your neighborhood organization. What is a data card? What size should I buy? › The data card stores the video clips so that you do not need to pay a monthly subscription to store video clips "in the cloud". You need one data card per camera. They are not very expensive so get a 256GB, class 10 data card like the SanDisk Extreme PRO. Can I enroll more than one camera with AwareNet? › Yes! Please do. Should I enroll my indoor security cameras with AwareNet? › No! Please enroll only OUTDOOR cameras with AwareNet. There is already someone on my block with a camera enrolled. Would it help to have another? › Yes! The technology is good but not flawless. A second camera, especially one located on the opposite side of the street, can be very beneficial — if the first camera misses something, the second one may catch it. How do I configure my camera to automatically send alert event information to AwareNet? › Start with the Camera Configuration page. How much of my internet data is this going to use? › We have designed the AwareNet system to use very little of your internet data but it depends on your specific camera and its configuration. The Reolink cameras will store the video clips locally on the card in the camera. If you follow our camera configuration guide, clips sent to AwareNet will be about 3MB in size and it will send videos only when it has detected a person between the hours of 10pm and 6am. Most nights, that will happen only once or twice. Note: Some cameras offer additional features which will increase use of your internet data. What information is AwareNet collecting about each alert event? › Each alert sent to AwareNet contains the camera name or id, the date/time of the event, the type of event (i.e. person or vehicle), and the video clip of the event. What does AwareNet do with the information collected? › The information for each event is stored in the AwareNet system for later reference and reporting. The information may be shared with the public, law enforcement, city officials and neighborhood organizations as appropriate to support neighborhood safety and security. Camera owners still have complete control of the video copy stored on their camera. In addition, AwareNet has the ability to send real-time notifications to appropriate persons (e.g. safety committee volunteers or a professional security guard) to review the event information and take action as appropriate. How long does AwareNet keep the information collected? › Typically, video clips are deleted after 15 days unless the event is categorized as unusual, suspicious, or criminal. Other than the video, data about an event is kept indefinitely for analysis and reporting. Who decides where the cameras are located? › Each camera owner decides where to place their camera. See the Camera Location page for some tips to consider. Should I contact AwareNet if I move my camera or change where it is pointed? › Yes. Please contact us if the camera has been: Relocated from the street to the alley or vice versa. Pointed in a different direction (N, NE, E, SE, S, SW, W, NW). Relocated to a different street address. Should I contact AwareNet if I change the setup or configuration of my camera? › Yes. Please contact us if you change any of the following: Camera Name Email address you use to automatically send alerts to AwareNet. Who has access to the live video for each camera? › Access to live video for each camera is controlled by the owner (typically using the smartphone app for their camera). Live video is not made available to AwareNet and is not available using AwareNet. Will I be notified if my camera recorded criminal activity? › Currently, camera owners are not automatically notified of criminal activity recorded by their camera. Can I get real-time alerts from other AwareNet cameras in my neighborhood? › No. The AwareNet system is not designed or intended to send residents real-time alerts. It is designed to send real-time alerts to a designated set of contacts (e.g. safety committee volunteers or a professional security guard) responsible for reviewing the alert and taking the appropriate action. What about privacy? › Like any home security camera, it is the responsibility of the camera owner to ensure the camera is not recording something private. We ask that you point your cameras at public settings such as the street in front of your house or the alley behind your house. Many cameras have a "privacy masking" feature that allows you to "black out" areas in the field of view that should not be seen or recorded. Please discuss your camera location with your neighbors and show them a sample of what will and will not be visible to the camera. It is best to be open and collaborative with your neighbors. The camera owner controls the audio recording. Audio can be very helpful to the people monitoring the alerts sent to AwareNet but audio is not required. Can these cameras "read" a license plate? › No. The cameras do not have automatic license plate capture capability. If a vehicle stops in the field of view, it may be possible to determine the license plate by reviewing the video clip. Do these cameras have "facial recognition" technology? › No. Not at this time. But facial recognition technology is starting to appear in residential cameras such as these. The AwareNet system is not designed to use facial recognition technology even if cameras enrolled with AwareNet start using it — and it is the camera owner who would decide whether it is used on their camera. How does the alert process actually work? › See the Alert Process page for a full explanation. Can I stop participating with AwareNet? › Yes. Participation is completely voluntary. At any time, the camera owner can turn off the notifications sent to AwareNet or simply turn off the camera. What does a designated reviewer see when they get an alert? › The reviewer uses a tablet or laptop to access the AwareNet web app . Where is AwareNet available? › AwareNet is available for use anywhere in the United States. On a Reolink Duo (version 1) camera, how do I watch or download the high resolution version of a recorded video clip? › As indicated by the

title: Reolink Camera Configuration
content: Reolink Camera Configuration The screenshot below shows a typical main Settings page from the Reolink iOS app for a Reolink camera. Your menu may be different depending on your camera and other factors. At the top of the Settings page, next to a picture of the camera, it shows: The name you have given the camera (e.g. 123 Main Street) The model of the camera (e.g. Reolink Duo 2 WiFi) The amount used and capacity of the data card in the camera Click on the camera name to display the Device Info page Information for each of the other options is listed below. In some cases, a link is provided to see more details about the option. When appropriate, an option is identified as CRITICAL or Important . Please give these extra attention. Wi-Fi Selecting your WiFi Network. Once this is set during the initial camera setup, you probably don't need to do anything here. This does not apply for PoE cameras. Display Optional. All things related to what you see on live and recorded video. Sounds Optional. Control whether audio is recorded with the video and control the volume for the speaker (used for you talking to someone and for the alert siren). Lights Optional. Control use of floodlights, spotlights, and infrared lights. Detection Alarm Important. Control the Sensitivity, Delay time, and Object Size for motion of Persons, Vehicles, and Animals. More details → Camera Recording CRITICAL. Control what is recorded to the data card in the camera (or, optionally, to the cloud). More details → Push Notifications Optional. It controls which events/alerts appear on your smartphone. There are separate settings (see Email Alerts below) for which events/alerts are sent to AwareNet. Many camera owners choose to leave this turned off. Email Alerts CRITICAL! This option controls which events/alerts are sent to AwareNet. This must be set correctly. There are separate settings (Camera Recording) to control which events/alerts are recorded for you to see later. More details → FTP Optional. Used by "techies" to automatically copy video clips to a separate storage server. Siren Optional. Control if and when a siren sound is activated. This feature can be useful as an additional deterrent, but may annoy nearby neighbors. Instead of triggering a default siren sound from the camera according to a schedule, it can play a 5 second recording / announcement (e.g. "Alert, your activity is subject to real-time monitoring and response"). Share Device Optional. Displays the UID (unique camera id) and a QR code which is a digital representation of the UID. To give someone access to your camera, you must give them either the UID or the QR code, plus a userid and a password. Advanced Optional. Manage access to the camera and set the date/time.

title: Review Alert
content: Review Alert How the designated reviewer monitors and acts on alerts. Alert List The main page for the reviewer is a list of alerts. The list automatically updates with the newest alerts at the top of the list. The reviewer can filter the list to suit their needs (e.g. Time/Date, Street Name, Event, Category). Reviewing an Alert Selecting an alert on the main page displays the review page for that alert. The address is a link to display Google Maps for that address. The compass direction in parenthesis is the direction the camera is facing. The reviewer categorizes each alert they review (i.e. False, Unremarkable, Unusual, Suspicious, Criminal). A comment is required for Unusual, Suspicious, and Criminal categories. The purple and blue buttons are quick navigation to other alerts (Previous On Block, Next On Block, Previous In Neighborhood, Next In Neighborhood).

title: Camera Brands and Models
content: Camera Brands and Models Every security camera must be powered and connected to a network. When selecting a camera, there are tradeoffs to consider based on how it is powered and how it is connected to a network. The first table below shows the available combinations of power and connection. POWER CONNECTION Cellular WiFi Ethernet Battery + Solar ✓ ✓ Plug In ✓ ✓ Ethernet ✓ The next table below shows the relative rating for each power and connection option. CATEGORY OPTION ACQUISITION $ OPERATING $ RELIABILITY PERFORMANCE DEPLOYMENT Power Battery + Solar High Low Low Low Fast Plug In Med Low Med High Varies Ethernet Low Low High High Varies Connection Cellular High High Low Low Fast WiFi Med Low Med Med Varies Ethernet Low Low High High Varies ↔️ Scroll to see all columns Below is the current list of cameras that do meet the requirements to work with AwareNet. We do our best to keep this information accurate but it is not guaranteed. Other camera brands/models will be added to the list as soon as we test them. To request support for a camera not listed below, tell us the brand and model of the camera. The minimum requirements for a camera are: The ability to identify motion as a person, vehicle, or something else. The ability to send an email notification or webhook to AwareNet (when a motion recording is captured). The email/webhook must include (1) the camera name or id (2) type of motion (person or vehicle), and (3) the video clip. Axis Available at www.axis.com . All models using Axis OS 11 or above should be compatible with AwareNet. Typical price is $600 - $5,000 per camera. Feel free to contact us with your questions. Reolink Available at www.reolink.com and www.amazon.com . All current models for sale by Reolink should be compatible with AwareNet. Typical price is $100 - $200 per camera. If you have a standard outlet available to power a camera, the Reolink Duo 3 WiFi is most often the best option. It is suitable for locating on a house or garage up to 35 feet from the street or alley. You will also need a 256GB class 10 data card. The Elite Floodlight WiFi is a good option if you need/want to really light up a dark area. If you can do Power over Ethernet (PoE), the Reolink Duo 3 PoE is an excellent option. If you must have a battery-powered camera, the Argus 4 Pro camera with matching solar panel is a good option. It is suitable for locating adjacent to the street or alley. It is not a good choice for installing at a location that is more than 10 feet from the sidewalk/street or areas with continuous people/vehicle traffic. You will also need a 256GB class 10 data card. Many other models are available for specific situations. Feel free to contact us with your questions. Unsupported Cameras Below is the current list of commonly available cameras that do not meet the requirements to work with AwareNet. We do our best to keep this information accurate but it is not guaranteed. Amcrest The Amcrest cameras with AI person & vehicle detection have the ability to send email alerts (but not webhook alerts). However, there is no option to include the video clip with the email. So the cameras do not meet the minimum requirements to work with AwareNet. Blink Unfortunately, Blink cameras do not meet the minimum requirements to work with AwareNet because they cannot send an email or webhook to AwareNet when the camera senses motion. Nest The Nest Outdoor camera can send an email (but not a webhook) when the camera senses motion. However, the only option is to send the email to the email account the camera is registered to. Depending on what email account the camera is registered to, it might be possible to configure that account to automatically forward the alerts to AwareNet. If you have a Nest Outdoor camera and would like to try this, please contact us. Ring Unfortunately, Ring cameras do not meet the minimum requirements to work with AwareNet because they cannot send an email or webhook to AwareNet when the camera senses motion. SimpliSafe The SimpliSafe camera with person detection has the ability to send email alerts. However, there is no option to include the video clip with the email. And it cannot send a webhook. So the camera does not meet the minimum requirements to work with AwareNet. Wyze Unfortunately, Wyze cameras do not meet the minimum requirements to work with AwareNet because they cannot send an email or webhook to AwareNet when the camera senses motion.

title: Tablet Setup
content: Tablet Setup Basic instructions for setting up a tablet for use with AwareNet. Requirement: Apple iPad required Download the complete setup guide for detailed instructions on configuring your iPad for use with the AwareNet reviewer web application. 📄 Download Tablet Setup Guide (PDF)
